[210:24]should get along with Russia. And I love that about the Trump administration. I don't trust

[210:35]And I've been kind of red-pilled on Russia over the years. I used to think it was based and edgy to say we're pro Russia

[210:54]away from China.

[210:57]They don't trust each other.

[211:50]the war is a core US interest to reestablish strategic stability with Russia, which I think

[214:40]So this is great too. Again, I am broadly supportive of this and I'll break this down

[214:46]very simply. So the foundation of what we call geopolitics, the modern study of geography

[214:55]and you could call it foreign policy, grand strategy, geo strategy. All of these fields

[215:08]They're called the heartland theory and the rimlan theory, the heartland theory by Mckinder

[215:13]and the rimlan theory by Spikeman.

[215:16]And what these two different theories say is that when you look at a map of the world,

[215:21]what you see is one giant world island.

[215:30]When you look at a map of the whole world stretched out,

[215:33]you see one supermassive landmass,

[215:36]which includes Europe, Asia, and Africa,

[215:39]and then a giant ocean.

[215:42]And under this idea,

[215:44]North America and South America are considered big islands

[215:47]like Australia.

[215:49]Why is that?

[215:51]Because if one country or one empire

[215:54]was able to marshal the resources

[215:56]of the Eurasian African landmass,

[216:00]Like say, for example, the Mongols, the Russians, what the Nazis dreamt of at one point.

[216:07]If one terrestrial empire, a land-based empire were to control the resources in Europe, the

[216:15]resources in Asia, the resources in Africa.

[216:22]Fertile soil, like the bread basket of Europe, the Baku oil fields, the Persian Gulf, the

[216:27]critical minerals in China and Africa.

[216:29]If one country can control all of that, that empire would be undefeatable, unbeatable.

[216:37]And there are more resources in Eurasia, Africa than in North and South America.

[216:45]When you look at the foundation of geopolitics,

[216:49]regimes are going to try to get power

[216:53]in the middle of the Eurasian African continent.

[216:56]It says the key to unlocking the heartland

[216:58]is in Eastern Europe and it's in the Middle East.

[217:01]Because that's the gateway between the three.

[217:05]continents. So whoever can control that heartland, that's why they call it the heartland thesis.

[217:11]Whoever can control the heartland of the Eurasian African landmass will then project power

[217:15]across it. They'll control all those resources. They'll dominate the globe.

[217:27]The remland plays a much more important role.

[217:29]And what he calls the remland is the coastal region

[217:33]that cuts through the Eurasian African landmass.

[217:40]the Bar Al-Mandeb, the Indian Ocean, the Arabian Sea,

[217:44]all the way through to the South and East China Sea.

[217:52]they call it the remland

[217:53]because it's the rim of the heartland,

[217:57]It's because goods travel across naval routes.

[218:01]They travel across sea routes.

[218:03]And so whoever can control those critical choke points, where all the commerce is actually

[218:09]happening, where those resources are exchanged, it's not going to be by rail, it's going to

[218:13]be by sea.

[218:14]And if it's by sea, it's going to be through that corridor.

[218:24]And that's where goods are gonna be trafficked

[218:27]and that is actually gonna be the wealth of the heartland.

[218:29]And so whoever can control the rimland

[218:31]is actually gonna be the most powerful.

[218:34]And anyway, that's the foundation of geopolitics.

[219:31]We will not be entangled in alliances over there because we don't have to.

[219:43]power can't touch us, and if there's a balance of power over there, we can really recuse
